dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 12 responding for unconscious patient at 1703 East Broad Street.
Hi, I'm James with the rescue squad. Can you explain what's going on?
My husband collapsed in the bathroom-unresponsive. I called 911 right after.
No eye opening. Testing for verbal and motor response now.
No verbal response detected.
No motor response either. He is unresponsive with eye, verbal, and motor each at 1 of GCS 3.
Airway seems clear. Checking for breathing.
No spontaneous breathing. Let me check the chest for movement.
No breathing detected at all.
No pulse and blood pressure is unobtainable.
No pulse detected.
Respiratory rate is zero. He is not breathing.
Blood pressure is unmeasurable-no pulse or signs of circulation.
SpO2 is non-obtainable due to no pulse.
EKG shows no electrical activity-flatline, technically called asystole.
What is the patient's name and how long was he in the bathroom before you found him?
His name is John. He was in the bathroom for about 20-30 minutes before I found him there.
Did he take any medications or know any allergies? Or feel any symptoms before this?
He had a pacemaker implanted on April 1, but I'm not sure why. He had hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
He didn't mention any medications being taken.
This is a cardiac arrest. Initiating resuscitation per protocol 2-7.
RFD started CPR and BVM on the AED upon arrival. Let's maintain ventilation and manual compressions.
Vital signs remain unchanged. Zero pulse, zero breaths, and EKG shows no activity.
Starting intraosseous access to give drugs-let me prep the device.
Preparing four-lead EKG monitoring for detailed cardiac readings.
Still asystolic. Flatline remains on monitor.
IO access established via right humeral head. Ready to push epinephrine.
Administering 1mg of 1:10,000 epinephrine through IO access.
Maintaining 15 LPM oxygen delivery via BVM with ET tube on the way.
After medication, still flatline-no change in rhythm.
Intubation in progress-visualizing cords and waveform capno confirms proper placement.
Still no spontaneous breathing-ventilation continues with BVM.
Monitor shows no return of electrical activity-still asystole.
Autopulse experienced a failure-swapping to manual compressions.
Vitals remain unchanged-no breathing, no pulse.
EKG unchanged-continuing compressions and ventilations.
Retrieving new autopulse from supervisor-restarting with it now.
New autopulse running, EKG remains asystolic.
Administering second dose of 1mg epinephrine via IO access.
No improvement after second dose-monitor still flatline.
Continuing CPR with autopulse. No return of breaths, pulse, or activity.
Third epinephrine dose underway-based on protocol.
Still no activity on monitor-continuing compressions.
Initiating call to Medical Control for directive on how to proceed.
MCV orders to discontinue resuscitation-time of death recorded at 14:46.
Scene secured-1234 Rich Street handed off to Officer Urban B213.
Final checks completed-no change in status; asystole ongoing.
ET tube remains in place, waveform capno confirms position.
Still nothing on EKG. Proceeding for final dose.
Third dose of epinephrine given-per protocol.
Still no sign of return to organized rhythm. EKG flat.
Vital signs confirmed-no spontaneous breathing or pulse. EKG shows no rhythm.
Communicated with Medical Control. All interventions executed. Orders to cease resuscitation confirmed.
Scene turned over to law enforcement-Unit 213B1 reported on scene.
No transport planned due to lack of ROSC at any stage.
Collapse occurred in bathroom without notice-verified by family.
No current information on diet or fluids before incident-unknown.
Confirmed pacemaker and cardiomyopathy are medical history from family.
No spontaneous breaths-ventilation ongoing per direct BVM support.
Confirmed 0/0 at every check.
Consistent asystole throughout evaluations-no rhythm fluctuations.
Rhythm monitoring confirmed no cardiac activity at all times.
ET tube remained in place until directive provided to cease resuscitation.
CPR via autopulse used conditionally-mostly manual compressions after first failure.
EKG showed consistently flatline with no changes upon all dosing.
IO site maintained and used throughout for medication access-no complications.
Epinephrine given thrice-no response seen each time.
Glucose readings repeatedly at near zero-no changes seen.
Cardiac arrest protocol completed per guidelines and MCV-no further action.
Scene fully secured and documented-exit complete.
Final status matches initial: unconsciousness resulting in no recovery.
Final EKG showed same asystole-protocol ended at that point.
All EKG data confirmed absence of cardiac activity and per directed, no cardioversion.
Endotracheal support withdrawn only upon directive of Medical Control.
Oxygen saturation remains at zero as no pulse or circulation is ongoing.
No changes on glucose-value remains undetectable.
All actions taken via protocol and MCV-all decisions made under supervision.
Equipment stripped and scene ready.
Death time confirmed by MCV at 14:46. Case closed.
911 caller reported unconsciousness-confirmed as unresponsive at GCS 3.
No symptoms reported prior to incident-no prior calls to 911 of similar type.
Recent pacemaker implantation and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy entered into medical record.
Still no eye opening-documenting continued level of unresponsiveness.
Airway remained unobstructed throughout care and no changes seen.
Breathing absent except for BVM assisted by others on first scene.
No circulation detected throughout resuscitation-no signs of return.
All EKG data confirmed absence of cardiac activity and per directed, no cardioversion.
Continuing per protocol with awareness of implanted device-manual and autopulse maintained.
Autopulse restored-compressions under automatic control now.
EKG flatline-no new rhythms detected with or without automated CPR.
Epinephrine second dose given-response monitored closely in real time.
No change in respiratory status-patient remains apneic with BVM assisting.
Asystole remains-no return to any form of organized rhythm.
Correct tube placement confirmed-ventilation confirmed stable.
Still nothing on EKG. Preparing for final dose.
No visible wounds, abrasions, or petechiae-skin color findings consistent with apneic condition.
Pupils equal, round, and dilated-assessments limited due to unresponsiveness.
No signs of obstruction or drainage in oral or nasal cavities.
No audible breath or heart sounds due to absent circulation and respiration.
Abdomen flat with no rigidity or distension-no palpable masses.
Extremities do not move-no pulses or deformities noted.
Patient reported no pain at time of collapse. Pain is undetectable now in unresponsive state.
No changes to medical history-still no medications, no recent oral intake reported.
Final scan-absent respiration, absent pulse, EKG silence-no further resuscitation.
Confirming protocol followed in whole and decision points made as per MCV.
Scene secured-handoff completed without complications.
